repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1924 silent film, "The Luck o' the Foolish," starring Harry Langdon.

title_tesim: Brigitte Bardot in A Very Private Affair
caption_tesim: BARDOT AFLOAT.....Brigitte Bardot found one way to get away from it all when filming MGM's "A VERY PRIVATE AFFAIR" in Geneva was to take a boat out onto the lake. Here she is against the Quai Mont Blanc. The column of water in the background is Geneva's famed landmark, the Jet d'Eau."A VERY PRIVATE AFFAIR" starring Brigitte Bardot and Marcello Mastroianni, is a Progefi-Cipra-CCM production for world-wide release by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer. A Franco-Italian co-production it was produced by Jacques Bar and Christine Gouze-Renal, directed by Louis Malle. "A VERY PRIVATE AFFAIR" was filmed on location in Switzerland, France and Italy.

title_tesim: Pastoral Symphony and Dance of the Hours sequences from Fantasia
caption_tesim: Top: The magnificent black Pegasus and his snow-white mate gambol through the air with their young entourage, to the music of Beethoven's "Pastoral Symphony" in Walt Disney's animated classic "Fantasia." Below: Beasts in the ballet include a prancing pachyderm and graceful alligator, stepping to the strains of Amilcare Ponchielli's "Dance of the Hours." Re-released on the occasion of its 50th anniversary, "Fantasia" boasts a completely restored and enhanced print utilizing the original Leopold Stokowski-conducted score. In color by Technicolor, "Fantasia" is re-released by Buena Vista Pictures Distribution.

title_tesim: Ben Turpin publicity photograph used to promote film, The Golden Age of Comedy
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1926 silent film, "A Blonde's Revenge," starring Ben Turpin. Although a well-known publicity photograph of actor Ben Turpin, it does not appear in "The Golden Age of Comedy," nor does any film scene from "A Blonde's Revenge." Turpin, however, is quite well-represented with other clips in the film, within the section entitled, "Act V - The Great Actor."

title_tesim: Dorothy Howe in Cocoanut Grove
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: CAPES - are back in various versions for Hollywood's early summer fashion trend. Dorothy Howe in Paramount's "Cocoanut Grove" wears a daytime dress of brown jersey with an interesting belt of shiny gold kid. The neckline is collarless but cut in a shallow, scalloped "V" which is repeated at the hem of the dress. The sleeves are long and bloused below the elbow over a tight cuff. An unlined, circular cape of beige wool crepe fastens at one side of the neckline and is banded in mink. Brown accessories and a muff of mink complete the costume.

title_tesim: Pat O'Brien and Broderick Crawford in Slightly Honorable
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:Pat O'Brien and Broderick Crawford are "stopped" by the camera in this action picture of their vicious battle staged for a scene in Walter Wanger's "City for Sale". Director Tay Garnett spent three days photographing this punishing sequence and each man bore the burdens of the encounter for several days. Both O'Brien and Crawford were thoroughly beaten and shaken up while making the scene. Crawford almost knocked out himself when he missed a swing at O'Brien, slipped and cracked his head against a desk. In the picture, the pair are seen as law partners who set out to bare the graft in the state paving racket and become involved in two murders.

description_tesim: From the AFI listing: A May, 1946 Hollywood Reporter news item noted that producer Virginia Van Upp's husband, Ralph Nelson, also a producer, was set to work on the film, but his contribution to the released film has not been confirmed. Various news items in HR indicate that production on the film was interrupted many times throughout the summer and fall of 1946, and that Van Upp and Charles Vidor, the film's original director, were embroiled in a bitter dispute about the script. In April, 1946, Vidor, while working on the film "Gilda," sued Columbia for termination of his contract. The suit was part of a long-standing dispute with studio executives and personnel that spanned many years. Production on the film was suspended for approximately two weeks in mid-August, 1946, after, according to a HR news item, Van Upp collapsed on the set. Van Upp was removed from the picture, and a HR news item suggested that her troubles stemmed from player commitments that forced her to begin filming the picture before the script was completed. Helen Deutsch took over Van Upp's production chores and, during the two-week production shutdown, supervised a series of script revisions and additions. A September 18, 1946 HR news item, noted that Vidor then became involved in a major dispute with Deutsch, whose supervision he was reportedly refusing. On September 24, 1946, HR printed a statement issued by Columbia production chief Harry Cohn, in which Cohn stated "emphatically and clearly that Miss Helen Deutsch is the producer of the picture and that Mr. Charles Vidor is not." The statement also reveals that Deutsch had asked to be relieved of her duties, and that Cohn insisted that she remain on the film. Vidor's suit against Columbia went to trial in December, 1946, at which time, according to HR, Vidor testified that Cohn made derogatory remarks to him and vilified his wife, Doris Warner. According to a September, 1947 HR news item, Vidor lost his suit against Columbia, and Columbia then sued Vidor.

caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Prior to the filming of Walt Disney's Robin Hood for RKO Radio, Richard Todd, who stars in the title role, and several members of the production unit, paid a visit to Nottinghamshire, to absorb the atmosphere of the original Robin Hood haunts and retrace the footsteps of the famous 12th century outlaww..... At Papplewick, on the verge of the Sherwood Forest, Richard Todd, producer Perce Pearce and art director Carmen Dillon, inspected a cave out in the solid rock of a hillside, where Robin Hood is said to have stabled his horses, well hidden from the prying eyes of the Sheriff's men.

caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Prior to the filming of Walt Disney's Robin Hood, for RKO Radio, Richard Todd, who stars in the title role, and several members of the production unit, paid a visit to Nottinghamshire, to absorb the atmosphere of the original Robin Hood haunts and retrace the footsteps of the famous 12th Century outlaw....... At Edwinstowe, deep in the heart of the Sherwood Forest, stands one of the oldest trees in England, the Major or Queen Oak. It has a vast hollow trunk roomy enough to hold fourteen people and said to have been the trysting place of Maid Marian and Robin Hood. When Richard Todd visited the Robin Hood haunts in Sherwood Forest, Joseph Smith, forest guard, proved a mine of information on the 12th century outlaw and the many local spots connected with his adventurous life.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Bill Owen, Michael Hordern, and extras in The Story of Robin Hood
uniform_title_tesim: Story Of Robin Hood, The  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: While Scathelock (Michael Hordern) is chained in the stocks in Nottingham Square, two of the Sheriff of Nottingham's men hoist up the unfortunate Stutely (Bill Owen) inside the skin of the deer he has stolen from Sherwood Forest, in Walt Disney's all-live-action Technicolor production The Story of Robin Hood, produced in England for RKO Radio world distribution. Richard Todd is starred in title role and Joan Rice as Maid Marian.
